The single source of truth for what "a valid Knowledge bundle" means, defined as
behaviour rather than prose. It is the **spec corpus**: the format is pinned by these
fixtures and their normative results, not by whatever the implementation happens to
print. This keeps the spec implementation-independent — the (one) Elixir implementation
in `composable-beliefs` is held to the corpus, and any future port can be checked the
same way.

## The contract

For every fixture under `fixtures/`, a conformant validator must emit, in `--json`
mode, exactly the object recorded in `expected/<fixture>.json`:

```json
{ "ok": <bool>, "errors": [{"path","code"}...], "warnings": [{"path","code"}...] }
```

with `errors` and `warnings` sorted by `(code, path)`.

**Only the `(severity, code, path)` triples are normative.** Human-readable messages,
the bundle root path, and ordering beyond the defined sort are NOT part of the contract,
so an implementation is free to word findings differently. The stable `code` values are
documented in the `CB.Knowledge.Validate` moduledoc (`composable-beliefs`).

## Running

The corpus is asserted by `composable-beliefs`' ExUnit conformance test
(`test/cb/knowledge_conformance_test.exs`): for each fixture it runs the validator and
diffs the contract object against `expected/<fixture>.json`. From `composable-beliefs/`:

```sh
mix test test/cb/knowledge_conformance_test.exs
```

It resolves this corpus via `KNOWLEDGE_REPO` (default: the sibling `../knowledge`
checkout), and skips if the corpus isn't present.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Conformance suite

The single source of truth for what "a valid Knowledge bundle" means, defined as
behaviour rather than prose. It exists so the format can have **more than one
implementation** — the Python reference tools in `../tools/`, and the Elixir
`mix knowledge.*` tasks in CB's OKF integration layer (`composable-beliefs`), which
passes this suite — without the two silently drifting.

## The contract

For every fixture under `fixtures/`, a conformant validator must emit, in `--json`
mode, exactly the object recorded in `expected/<fixture>.json`:

```json
{ "ok": <bool>, "errors": [{"path","code"}...], "warnings": [{"path","code"}...] }
```

with `errors` and `warnings` sorted by `(code, path)`.

**Only the `(severity, code, path)` triples are normative.** Human-readable messages,
the bundle root path, and ordering beyond the defined sort are NOT part of the contract,
so implementations are free to word findings differently. The stable `code` values are
documented at the top of `../tools/validate.py`.

## Running

Against the Python reference implementation:

```sh
./conformance/run.sh
```

Against any other implementation, point `VALIDATE_CMD` at a command that accepts
`<bundle-root> --json` and prints the contract object:

```sh
VALIDATE_CMD="mix knowledge.validate" ./conformance/run.sh
```

The runner exits non-zero if any fixture's output differs from its expected file.

## Fixtures

`fixtures/valid/` must produce `ok: true`; `fixtures/invalid/` each isolate a single
hard-check failure so a divergence points straight at the responsible rule:

| Fixture | Asserts |
|---|---|
| `valid_minimal` | a minimal well-formed bundle passes |
| `valid_cb_ok` | CB-tier docs with ids + resolving deps pass with no warnings |
| `valid_id_format_warns` | a nonstandard id warns (`id_format_invalid`) but stays `ok: true` |
| `invalid_bad_type` | `type_not_in_taxonomy` |
| `invalid_weak_description` | `description_missing_or_short` |
| `invalid_placeholder` | `placeholder_in_frontmatter` |
| `invalid_status` | `invalid_status` |
| `invalid_timestamp` | `invalid_timestamp` |
| `invalid_broken_link` | `broken_link` |
| `invalid_cb_no_id` | `cb_tier_missing_id` |
| `invalid_dup_id` | `duplicate_id` |
| `invalid_stale_manifest` | `manifest_stale` |
| `invalid_missing_manifest` | `manifest_missing` |

## Changing the corpus

Fixtures and expected files are authored by `regen.py` so they can't drift from each
other by hand:

```sh
python3 conformance/regen.py
```

Review the resulting diff — each `expected/*.json` must reflect the fixture's *intended*
outcome, not merely whatever the current code happened to print.

input.new_string:
## Changing the corpus

Fixtures and their `expected/*.json` are maintained by hand. When adding or changing a
fixture, author both the bundle (including a fresh `manifest.json`, via
`mix knowledge.manifest <fixture>`) and its expected result, then run the conformance
test. Each `expected/*.json` must reflect the fixture's *intended* outcome — review it,
don't just paste whatever the validator currently prints.

A fixture is one isolated failure: keep `fixtures/invalid/*` to a single hard-check each,
so a divergence points straight at the responsible rule.

1	---
2	type: thread
3	title: 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed, built, and integrated with CB
4	description: Use when you need what happened in the session that created this standard and CB's OKF integration layer, without replaying it. Outcome: a conformance-tested OKF standard + a working CB↔OKF bridge.
5	tags: [meta, thread, design]
6	status: active
7	timestamp: 2026-06-21
8	id: meta:okf-session-2026-06-21
9	artifact: session:2026-06-21-okf-knowledge-design
10	---
11	
12	# 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed, built, and integrated with CB
13	
14	> **Provenance.** This is the *summary* (Layer 2). The raw verbatim transcript (Layer 1,
15	> immutable) is the byte-for-byte session log, now persisted as the paired `source` doc:
16	> [`2026-06-21-okf-cb-session-source.md`](2026-06-21-okf-cb-session-source.md) →
17	> [`meta/sessions/2026-06-21-okf-cb-design.jsonl`](sessions/2026-06-21-okf-cb-design.jsonl).
18	> Docs this session authored carry `threads: [meta:okf-session-2026-06-21]`; subsequent
19	> sessions append their ids.
20	
21	## What this session was
22	Evaluate whether to build a generalized, cross-repo knowledge base on the Open Knowledge
23	Format (OKF), how it relates to Karpathy's LLM-wiki and the existing Composable Beliefs
24	(CB) system, and how to centralize the currently-haphazard threads/plans/analyses. It
25	grew into building the standard, CB's integration layer, and the CB↔OKF bridge.
26	
27	## What was decided
28	- **OKF is the floor, CB is the opt-in ceiling** (two-tier). OKF is mostly a renaming of
29	  a pattern already reinvented in `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`. CB is a conceptual
30	  superset with a separate serialization — build an adapter, not a migration; CB's
31	  `beliefs.json` stays canonical.
32	- **The synthesis layer IS the wiki** — one body of OKF markdown both agent and human
33	  read; a rendered site is a throwaway view. Agent reads synthesis first, drills to sources.
34	- **`description` is a relevance hook + a generated `manifest.json`** give the
35	  skills-style "understand a doc without loading it" capability.
36	- **Identity: path-canonical, `id` additive.** Floor docs use path identity; CB-tier docs
37	  carry a stable `namespace:local` `id` (decoupled from path, survives renames).
38	- **`knowledge` is a standard, not an app** — defined by a conformance suite so multiple
39	  implementations can be checked for equivalence. It is a sister repo under `amieval/`.
40	- **CB is a conformant OKF consumer/dialect, not a format owner** (positioning recorded
41	  in `cb-direction`).
42	- **Threads persist as `source` (raw) + `thread` (summary)** with `threads:[]` provenance;
43	  the operational `/end` directive lives in CB's graph, the format rule in this standard.
44	
45	## What it produced
46	- **The standard** (`amieval/knowledge`, `ob6to8/knowledge`): [spec](../standard/KNOWLEDGE.md),
47	  [types](../standard/types.md), [frontmatter](../standard/frontmatter.md), [tiers](../standard/tiers.md),
48	  [ADOPT](../ADOPT.md), [CONVERT](../CONVERT.md), templates, `tools/build_manifest.py`,
49	  `tools/validate.py` (self-verifying, `--json`), and a [conformance suite](../conformance/README.md).
50	- **CB's OKF integration layer** (`composable-beliefs`): `mix knowledge.manifest` /
51	  `knowledge.validate` (byte-equal Elixir ports, pass all 13 fixtures) plus the bridge
52	  `mix knowledge.emit` (the 202-belief graph validates 0/0) and `mix knowledge.ingest`.
53	- The [layering analysis](okf-vs-cb-layering.md), the [two-tier position](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md),
54	  and the cb-direction note `2026-06-21-cb-as-conformant-okf-consumer.md`.
55	- The **soft id-format check** (`id_format_invalid`, warns) added to both implementations.
56	
57	## Open / next
58	- Use the OKF/Karpathy wiki as the prose baseline arm in the dag-vs-prose eval.
59	- Pilot CONVERT on a real store (e.g. `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`, including `[[wikilink]]`
60	  → path-link + `deps` transforms).
61	- Done: renamed `spec/` → `standard/` (folders denote role, not type).
62	- Next: assert the `/end` thread-persistence directive into CB's graph; wire the verbatim
63	  transcript-file duplication into `/end` (it copies the `.jsonl`, it does not reconstruct).
64

# 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed, built, and integrated with CB

> **Provenance.** This is the *summary* (Layer 2). The raw verbatim transcript (Layer 1,
> immutable) is the byte-for-byte session log, now persisted as the paired `source` doc:
> [`2026-06-21-okf-cb-session-source.md`](2026-06-21-okf-cb-session-source.md) →
> [`meta/sessions/2026-06-21-okf-cb-design.jsonl`](sessions/2026-06-21-okf-cb-design.jsonl).
> Docs this session authored carry `threads: [meta:okf-session-2026-06-21]`; subsequent
> sessions append their ids.

## What this session was
Evaluate whether to build a generalized, cross-repo knowledge base on the Open Knowledge
Format (OKF), how it relates to Karpathy's LLM-wiki and the existing Composable Beliefs
(CB) system, and how to centralize the currently-haphazard threads/plans/analyses. It
grew into building the standard, CB's integration layer, and the CB↔OKF bridge.

## What was decided
- **OKF is the floor, CB is the opt-in ceiling** (two-tier). OKF is mostly a renaming of
  a pattern already reinvented in `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`. CB is a conceptual
  superset with a separate serialization — build an adapter, not a migration; CB's
  `beliefs.json` stays canonical.
- **The synthesis layer IS the wiki** — one body of OKF markdown both agent and human
  read; a rendered site is a throwaway view. Agent reads synthesis first, drills to sources.
- **`description` is a relevance hook + a generated `manifest.json`** give the
  skills-style "understand a doc without loading it" capability.
- **Identity: path-canonical, `id` additive.** Floor docs use path identity; CB-tier docs
  carry a stable `namespace:local` `id` (decoupled from path, survives renames).
- **`knowledge` is a standard, not an app** — defined by a conformance suite so multiple
  implementations can be checked for equivalence. It is a sister repo under `amieval/`.
- **CB is a conformant OKF consumer/dialect, not a format owner** (positioning recorded
  in `cb-direction`).
- **Threads persist as `source` (raw) + `thread` (summary)** with `threads:[]` provenance;
  the operational `/end` directive lives in CB's graph, the format rule in this standard.

## What it produced
- **The standard** (`amieval/knowledge`, `ob6to8/knowledge`): [spec](../standard/KNOWLEDGE.md),
  [types](../standard/types.md), [frontmatter](../standard/frontmatter.md), [tiers](../standard/tiers.md),
  [ADOPT](../ADOPT.md), [CONVERT](../CONVERT.md), templates, `tools/build_manifest.py`,
  `tools/validate.py` (self-verifying, `--json`), and a [conformance suite](../conformance/README.md).
- **CB's OKF integration layer** (`composable-beliefs`): `mix knowledge.manifest` /
  `knowledge.validate` (byte-equal Elixir ports, pass all 13 fixtures) plus the bridge
  `mix knowledge.emit` (the 202-belief graph validates 0/0) and `mix knowledge.ingest`.
- The [layering analysis](okf-vs-cb-layering.md), the [two-tier position](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md),
  and the cb-direction note `2026-06-21-cb-as-conformant-okf-consumer.md`.
- The **soft id-format check** (`id_format_invalid`, warns) added to both implementations.

- Use the OKF/Karpathy wiki as the prose baseline arm in the dag-vs-prose eval.
- Pilot CONVERT on a real store (e.g. `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`, including `[[wikilink]]`
  → path-link + `deps` transforms).
- Done: renamed `spec/` → `standard/` (folders denote role, not type).
- Next: assert the `/end` thread-persistence directive into CB's graph; wire the verbatim
  transcript-file duplication into `/end` (it copies the `.jsonl`, it does not reconstruct).

# 2026-06-22 — Collapsed the tooling to a single Elixir implementation

> **Provenance.** Summary (Layer 2) of the session that audited this repo and executed
> the collapse. Pairing the byte-for-byte verbatim transcript as a `source` doc is an
> end-of-session step (see Open/next).

## What this session was
An audit of the `knowledge` repo (README/sitemap accuracy, doc/functionality drift,
design-intent clarity), which surfaced that the format had **two implementations** — the
Python reference tools here and the Elixir `mix knowledge.*` tasks in `composable-beliefs`
— and that the Elixir side was already a superset. The user chose to collapse to
Elixir-only.

## What was decided
- **One implementation, in Elixir.** The Python `tools/` were a duplicate parser +
  validator + manifest builder; the Elixir side already passes all 13 conformance
  fixtures and additionally carries the `emit`/`ingest` CB↔OKF bridge that Python never
  had. Cross-repo portability (the Python tools' one advantage) is not a requirement here.
- **Fold all executable tooling into `composable-beliefs`.** `knowledge/` becomes the
  standard only: spec docs + the conformance corpus + `beliefs/`. No code lives here.
- **Conformance moves from a bash runner to an ExUnit gate.** The whole cross-impl
  apparatus (`run.sh`'s `VALIDATE_CMD` indirection, `regen.py`) existed only to keep two
  implementations in sync. The corpus (`fixtures/` + `expected/*.json`) stays the SSOT;
  `composable-beliefs`' test asserts the one validator against it.
- **Residual kept on purpose.** `Validate.to_contract`/`--json` stays, because the ExUnit
  gate reuses it to diff against `expected/*.json`. Shedding it (asserting on raw
  `{errors, warnings}` structs) is a possible later simplification, not done here.

## What it produced
- **Deleted** (`knowledge/`): `tools/build_manifest.py`, `tools/validate.py`,
  `conformance/run.sh`, `conformance/regen.py`; (`composable-beliefs/`): `bin/knowledge-validate`.
- **Added**: `composable-beliefs/test/cb/knowledge_conformance_test.exs` — the gate;
  iterates this repo's fixtures (resolved via `KNOWLEDGE_REPO`, default sibling
  `../knowledge`) and diffs `to_contract` against `expected/*.json`. 13/13 pass.
- **Reframed docs to Elixir-canonical**: [README](../README.md), [CLAUDE](../CLAUDE.md),
  [ADOPT](../ADOPT.md), [CONVERT](../CONVERT.md), [spec §5](../standard/KNOWLEDGE.md),
  [conformance/README](../conformance/README.md). Commands are now
  `mix knowledge.manifest|validate <root>` run from `composable-beliefs/`.
- **Folded in three audit fixes** while rewriting those docs: `beliefs/` now documented in
  the README Layout + the OKF-side/CB-side relationship made explicit (closing a
  design-intent gap); the `beliefs/`-is-not-an-OKF-bundle footgun called out in CLAUDE;
  two dead `#tiers` README anchors repointed to `standard/tiers.md`; stale `spec/` tool
  docstrings retired with the tools.

## Open / next
- Pair the verbatim transcript of this session as a `source` doc (the `/end` step), and
  append `meta:elixir-collapse-2026-06-22` to the `threads:[]` of docs it touched.
- Optional: port fixture authoring to a `mix knowledge.conformance.regen` task (replacing
  the deleted `regen.py`); for now fixtures are hand-maintained.
- Optional: drop `to_contract`/`--json` and assert directly on `{errors, warnings}`.
- The historical [2026-06-21 design-session thread](2026-06-21-okf-cb-design-session.md)
  still names the Python tools — left intact as an immutable record of what that session
  produced; this thread records their later removal.

# Meta — the design record

The **foundational, authoritative record** of how this methodology was conceived and
architected. Not demo data: this is the repo applying its own format to its own real
history, exactly as `ADOPT.md` prescribes every adopting repo keep a `meta/` for
threads, analyses, and decisions about itself. Read these to understand *why* the spec
is shaped the way it is before changing it.

It also incidentally shows the floor↔ceiling boundary in real use: a `thread` and an
`analysis` on the OKF floor, plus a CB-tier `position`.

## Contents
- [2026-06-21-okf-cb-design-session.md](2026-06-21-okf-cb-design-session.md) — *thread*: the session that produced this repo.
- [okf-vs-cb-layering.md](okf-vs-cb-layering.md) — *analysis*: how OKF, Karpathy, and CB layer.
- [two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md) — *position* (CB tier): the floor/ceiling decision.

The collapse is done and verified. The only remaining tool-name mentions are inside the new collapse thread doc, which deliberately records what was deleted.

## Done — Elixir-only collapse

**Deleted** (the duplicate Python impl + cross-impl scaffolding):
- `knowledge/tools/build_manifest.py`, `tools/validate.py` (and `__pycache__/`, `tools/`)
- `knowledge/conformance/run.sh`, `conformance/regen.py`
- `composable-beliefs/bin/knowledge-validate`

**Added** (the replacement gate):
- `composable-beliefs/test/cb/knowledge_conformance_test.exs` — iterates the standard's fixtures (resolved via `KNOWLEDGE_REPO`, default sibling `../knowledge`) and diffs `Validate.to_contract` against `expected/*.json`. **13/13 pass.** Skips gracefully if the corpus isn't checked out.

**Docs reframed Elixir-canonical** — README, CLAUDE, ADOPT, CONVERT, `standard/KNOWLEDGE.md` §5, `conformance/README.md`: commands are now `mix knowledge.manifest|validate <root>` run from `composable-beliefs/`; no tooling is copied into adopting repos.

**Three audit fixes folded into the same pass:** `beliefs/` now in the README Layout with the OKF-side/CB-side relationship spelled out (closes the design-intent gap); the "don't run OKF tools on `beliefs/`" footgun called out in CLAUDE; the two dead `#tiers` anchors repointed to `standard/tiers.md`; the stale `spec/` docstrings retired with the tools.

**Decision recorded** (dogfood): `meta/2026-06-22-elixir-only-collapse.md` (`type: thread`), added to `meta/index.md`, manifest regenerated.

### Verification
- `mix compile` clean; conformance gate 13/13; `knowledge_test.exs` + conformance = 21/21.
- `mix knowledge.validate ../knowledge/demo` → 0/0; `../knowledge/meta` → 0 errors (2 expected cross-bundle dep warnings); `meta` manifest `--check` fresh.
- Bridge intact: `mix knowledge.emit /tmp/kn-out` (204 beliefs) → validates 0/0.
- No live python/tool references remain outside the historical + new thread docs; README links resolve; 0 dead anchors.

### Two notes
1. **Pre-existing unrelated failure:** `mix test` has 1 failure — `schema_contracts_test.exs:200`, where CB belief `cb:c043` is `superseded` but the test expects `active`. It's in CB's own belief graph, untouched by this work. Flagging so you can address it separately.
2. **Not done, by policy:** I did **not** commit anything (two repos: `knowledge` deletions+docs, `composable-beliefs` test add + bin delete — your call when to commit). I also **skipped the optional follow-up belief** in `knowledge/beliefs/beliefs.json` (port `regen`, optionally shed `to_contract`) — writing to `beliefs/` needs your explicit go-ahead per CB's data-protection rule. Want me to author it via the CB write flow (`mix cb.preflight`/`import`)?

content.content:
=== Q1: the doc ===
type: thread
title: 2026-06-22 — Collapsed the tooling to a single Elixir implementation

=== find /end skill ===

=== dotfiles-claude repo? ===
/Users/mark/.claude
origin	https://github.com/ob6to8/dotfiles-claude.git (fetch)
origin	https://github.com/ob6to8/dotfiles-claude.git (push)
5d485b4 end skill step 5: duplicate the real session .jsonl (implements cb:a518)
9bd6302 skills/end: step 2 gains direction routing - strategy/positioning/competitor content never lands on public surfaces; routes to cb-direction or amieval-direction by subject (the only two destinations, hardcoded); step 6 drafts get the matching guard
1c73952 skills/end: step 5 gains the chronicle - narrative thread summaries for operator steering (cb:a520)

=== grep cb graph for CLAUDE.md generation / digest / thread-persistence directives ===
"claim": "Contracts are bound to domain, not implementation - they survive code rewrites. A domain expert can author them without understanding code; a coding agent can implement against them without understanding business context. Contracts generate tests, not the other way around: if you rewrote a module from scratch, every test breaks but the contract stays the same."
"claim": "When the operator relays a verbal or informal external communication (a text message, phone call, in-person conversation) that causes a field to be written, the provenance entry uses source user:<operator>:<date> (the existing authorship prefix from c030) and the verbatim quote or paraphrase is recorded in the note field. No new source type is introduced. If the communication is later confirmed by a cached artifact (email thread, calendar event), a follow-up provenance entry cites that artifact and notes that it corroborates the prior attestation."
"claim": "Plans should encode intent, not implementation. Intent lives in the DAG as beliefs; current state is assessed at execution time against the live codebase; implementation sequence is generated fresh when work is picked up, from intent + current state. Static implementation steps in plans are liabilities because the world drifts between plan authoring and plan execution. A plan file survives only if it encodes sequencing that cannot be generated - actual choreography across sessions, or constraints not derivable from current state. Everything else is a DAG assertion."
"claim": "A digest file that caches active implications, regenerated by a command, has freshness that depends on procedural enforcement (a skill tells agents to regenerate after writes). Agents can and do forget, producing a stale digest that is then read as authoritative by subsequent sessions. Persisted caches of graph-derived content whose freshness is procedural rather than structural are antipatterns; they embed the staleness risk they were meant to solve. The elimination path: fold active-implication loading into a session-start belief query and render the digest live from the graph instead of from disk."
"claim": "An artifact is an external referent — a file, gmail thread, session log, user statement, web page, or other addressable thing outside the belief graph. Artifacts are identified by typed URIs (per c040) and cited from beliefs via the artifact field."
"claim": "A belief carries two distinct relations to other entities. `deps` is belief-to-belief logical derivation: the deps' claims together justify the current belief's claim; required on type:compound and type:implication, absent on type:primitive. `subjects` is belief-to-entity topical reference: what the belief is about, including artifacts (files, threads, URLs), code modules, and sometimes other beliefs. A belief can be about another belief without depending on it, and can depend on another belief without being about it."
"claim": "The audit chain for architectural decisions runs: code -> contract -> graph -> plan/recap metadata -> raw conversation. The bottom rung is currently ephemeral, single-machine, prunable transcript files. Lossy synthesis is sufficient for most plans but may be inadequate for architecturally load-bearing decisions where the audit needs to reach the raw negotiation. Open question: when (if ever) is durable-in-version-control verbatim conversation worth the storage and maintenance cost?"
"claim": "For a belief graph to earn its keep operationally, beliefs must be queryable at session start and before writes - not only authored. Compiling beliefs into a static surface (a generated guidance file) is a once-per-session load, not a contextual query at decision time. Until contextual queries exist, the graph functions as developer-facing structure: valuable, but short of the operational claim."
"claim": "Active behavioral directives are not cached to a digest file; load them live from the graph by querying active implications (`mix bs list implication`). Per cb:a386, a digest whose freshness depends on remembering to regenerate it is an antipattern that embeds the staleness it was meant to solve - render live from the DAG instead."
"claim": "Verify with the graph's own tooling: `mix cb.verify.schema` checks a collection against the contracts it carries, `mix cb.verify.collection <namespace>` checks it together with its declared dependency collections, and `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check` confirms this file is current."
"claim": "The framework CLAUDE.md compiles from the beliefs listed in this contract's render_sections. The file is read-only and regenerated from the cb: graph by `mix cb.generate.claude_md`. Authoring happens by creating beliefs (via /assert) and by editing this contract's render_sections; hand-edits to CLAUDE.md are overwritten on the next generation."
"claim": "Contracts are never compiled into code: spec-to-code generation is nondeterministic and unmaintainable. The contract-to-code relationship is verification, not generation - interpreters query a contract and the test suite asserts the code conforms (the `verify_against_contract` pattern, cb:a417 / cb:a425). Drift surfaces as a failing test, not a regenerated module."
"claim": "Generated prose (CLAUDE.md, rule files) compiles from the graph wherever there is an oracle to diff against, and its freshness is enforced structurally in CI - a gate fails on drift - not procedurally, per cb:a386. Hand-written prose is reserved for a distinct audience, the front-door README, and must never freeze graph-derived facts such as counts or lists, which drift; it points at the live source instead."
"claim": "The framework CLAUDE.md compiles from the beliefs listed in this contract's render_sections. The file is read-only and regenerated from the cb: graph by `mix cb.generate.claude_md`. Authoring happens by creating beliefs (via /assert) and by editing this contract's render_sections; hand-edits to CLAUDE.md are overwritten on the next generation."
"claim": "Active behavioral directives are not cached to a digest file; load them live from the graph by querying active directives (`mix bs list directive`). Per cb:a386, a digest whose freshness depends on remembering to regenerate it is an antipattern that embeds the staleness it was meant to solve - render live from the DAG instead."
"claim": "The framework CLAUDE.md compiles from the beliefs listed in this contract's render_sections. The file is read-only and regenerated from the cb: graph by `mix cb.generate.claude_md`. Authoring happens by creating beliefs (via /assert) and by editing this contract's render_sections; hand-edits to CLAUDE.md are overwritten on the next generation."
"claim": "The cb ecosystem migrated to the four-type v2 schema on 2026-06-10: the framework graph (76 nodes re-typed, the c026/c027/c029/c031/c032/c038 contract family superseded by c051-c056, new contracts c057/c058/c059, CLAUDE.md render beliefs superseded and regenerated) and all seven sibling collections (codepath, method, sdl, toy, lib, agent-behavior, paradigm) via mix cb.migrate.v2, with adjudicated splits sdl:a006 -> a008+a009, toy:a9 -> a10+a11, the D3 trim sdl:a3 -> a010, and six agent-behavior mood re-homes (a396-a401). All eight collections verify under v2; sdl reproduces exactly its two teaching method-check failures."
"claim": "Persistence ceremony is proportionate to stance. A position document earns its keep when the reasoning is the artifact - a stance whose verbatim wording matters - and its claims are then extracted into the graph. Observations with an obvious prescription skip the document and go straight into the graph as directives, the gap carried as rationale prose or a small design-gap node underneath, stipulated by the thread that surfaced them. If every wart became a three-artifact pipeline, the pipeline would stop being read."
"claim": "Live agent work is observable through a lap log: a scratch markdown file (tmp/) the operator keeps open in an editor split, appended station by station as work proceeds. Entries follow the anchor discipline: the content anchor is the stored truth, the line number is a convenience snapshot resolved at write time and never copied forward, and any station that rewrites a file re-emits fresh locators on its way out. The log is scaffolding - whatever it surfaces that matters graduates to the transcript or the graph at lap end."
"claim": "Build the belief-card renderer: a mix task rendering any belief as a readable markdown mirror (claim, grounding, evidence, deps as resolved links) with a write-time locator back to its JSON definition - the middle tier of the in-document reference convention (gloss, card, substrate). Cards are derived views, regenerated on demand, never authoritative; the JSON is the graph."
"claim": "Settle the in-document belief-reference convention for glue surfaces: every cb reference carries an italic gloss (enough meaning to keep reading), a link to its rendered card, and a link to the JSON definition at a write-time-resolved line. Settle it through use in the transcript/lap-log surfaces, then encode the settled form as a convention directive depping on a497."
"claim": "Author obligations against the live graph, never from session memory: before minting a backlog node, pull the repos and query the desk (unlinked + lifecycle:discrete) plus the work's artifacts. Session memory, digests, and other caches are orientation; the graph is the SSOT of what remains. Multiple sessions write these repos concurrently, and a memory snapshot rots the moment another thread pushes."
"claim": "A backlog directive is self-bootstrapping: an agent reading it cold must be able to reach everything needed to begin - the vision, the conventions, the prior work - through the directive's deps and resolvable artifacts (document:/plan:). A session: stipulation is a dead end for a fresh agent until the session-persistence pipeline lands; pair it with a document: pointer in evidence to the transcript or position where the stipulation is readable."
"claim": "A session resumes from the graph, never from a handoff: pull the repos, query the desk (`mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete`) for live obligations and `mix bs stale --cascade` for review flags, then follow each directive's deps and document:/plan: artifacts to the records that ground it. Trust the graph over any memory, digest, or note - other sessions write these repos concurrently."
"claim": "The framework CLAUDE.md compiles from the beliefs listed in this contract's render_sections. The file is read-only and regenerated from the cb: graph by `mix cb.generate.claude_md`. Authoring happens by creating beliefs (via /assert) and by editing this contract's render_sections; hand-edits to CLAUDE.md are overwritten on the next generation."
"claim": "Agent session memory is an ephemeral cache, never a store: project and work state is banned from it - the graph owns obligations, the repos own records, CLAUDE.md compiles the bootstrap. At session end, promote anything load-bearing through the write flow, then prune; what survives between sessions is at most a thin operator-preferences layer, and only until a private overlay collection gives preferences a graph home. The a386 doctrine applied to the agent's own notebook: a cache whose freshness is procedural embeds the staleness it was meant to solve."
"claim": "Author the /position capture skill (skills/position/): run at the moment a thread settles something concrete, it applies the a496 ceremony gate (position document only when the verbatim reasoning is the artifact; otherwise route to /assert), stages a position file in the host repo's positions/ - verbatim passages, claims with intended types, **Anchor:** lines verified via the draft-mode resolver, Class header, Origin citing the session - and never writes beliefs/ directly; extraction stays with /assert. Belief-ref rendering follows the a503 convention. The 2026-06-10 thread is the worked trace, including the failure modes the rules must guard."
"claim": "Emit belief ids in compiled output: CB.OutputTarget renders render_sections claims bare (render_section/3, lib/cb/output_target.ex), so a compiled digest like CLAUDE.md strips line-level provenance and anything quoting it cites the rendering instead of the graph. "claim": "Make session: artifacts resolvable: evidence cites descriptive slugs (session:2026-06-10-substrate-position-discussion) that dereference to nothing, while the transcripts they name exist on disk as uuid-keyed jsonl files under ~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project>/. "claim": "Every thread that does substantive work persists twice at close: a transcript (the condensed record, co-located with its plan set or the thread's center of gravity) and a chronicle (chronicles/, dated) - a prose narrative for the operator: where things stood, the arc with its incidents as story beats, where things stand now, and what the next session inherits. The transcript serves the audit, the graph serves the work, the chronicle serves the steering; in a chronicle, narrative carries the load and ids stay subordinate - the inverse of the receipts register."

"claim": "Contracts are bound to domain, not implementation - they survive code rewrites. A domain expert can author them without understanding code; a coding agent can implement against them without understanding business context. Contracts generate tests, not the other way around: if you rewrote a module from scratch, every test breaks but the contract stays the same."
"claim": "When the operator relays a verbal or informal external communication (a text message, phone call, in-person conversation) that causes a field to be written, the provenance entry uses source user:<operator>:<date> (the existing authorship prefix from c030) and the verbatim quote or paraphrase is recorded in the note field. No new source type is introduced. If the communication is later confirmed by a cached artifact (email thread, calendar event), a follow-up provenance entry cites that artifact and notes that it corroborates the prior attestation."
"claim": "Plans should encode intent, not implementation. Intent lives in the DAG as beliefs; current state is assessed at execution time against the live codebase; implementation sequence is generated fresh when work is picked up, from intent + current state. Static implementation steps in plans are liabilities because the world drifts between plan authoring and plan execution. A plan file survives only if it encodes sequencing that cannot be generated - actual choreography across sessions, or constraints not derivable from current state. Everything else is a DAG assertion."
"claim": "A digest file that caches active implications, regenerated by a command, has freshness that depends on procedural enforcement (a skill tells agents to regenerate after writes). Agents can and do forget, producing a stale digest that is then read as authoritative by subsequent sessions. Persisted caches of graph-derived content whose freshness is procedural rather than structural are antipatterns; they embed the staleness risk they were meant to solve. The elimination path: fold active-implication loading into a session-start belief query and render the digest live from the graph instead of from disk."
"claim": "An artifact is an external referent — a file, gmail thread, session log, user statement, web page, or other addressable thing outside the belief graph. Artifacts are identified by typed URIs (per c040) and cited from beliefs via the artifact field."
"claim": "A belief carries two distinct relations to other entities. `deps` is belief-to-belief logical derivation: the deps' claims together justify the current belief's claim; required on type:compound and type:implication, absent on type:primitive. `subjects` is belief-to-entity topical reference: what the belief is about, including artifacts (files, threads, URLs), code modules, and sometimes other beliefs. A belief can be about another belief without depending on it, and can depend on another belief without being about it."
"claim": "The audit chain for architectural decisions runs: code -> contract -> graph -> plan/recap metadata -> raw conversation. The bottom rung is currently ephemeral, single-machine, prunable transcript files. Lossy synthesis is sufficient for most plans but may be inadequate for architecturally load-bearing decisions where the audit needs to reach the raw negotiation. Open question: when (if ever) is durable-in-version-control verbatim conversation worth the storage and maintenance cost?"
"claim": "For a belief graph to earn its keep operationally, beliefs must be queryable at session start and before writes - not only authored. Until contextual queries exist, the graph functions as developer-facing structure: valuable, but short of the operational claim."

Looking at the test failure, c043 is marked as superseded by c066, but the test expects c043 to have an "active" status. This suggests either the test is outdated and should reference c066 instead, or c043 was incorrectly superseded. The test itself indicates c043 supersedes c040, so now c066 appears to be the current active enum-registry contract in the chain.

I need to check the test around line 197-210 and examine c066 to understand what's happening. The fix should be in the test itself—updating it to assert c066 is active rather than c043, rather than modifying the beliefs to undo the supersession.

On the CLAUDE.md generation policy: there's a contract (c061) that defines how CLAUDE.md gets generated from a DAG output-target's render_sections, tagged with `output:claude-md`. This is a generic framework that can target any output-target. However, the knowledge repo's CLAUDE.md doesn't have a corresponding output-target contract, so it's currently hand-written rather than graph-compiled, unlike the framework's CLAUDE.md which is generated from the graph.

I hand-edited knowledge/CLAUDE.md, which technically doesn't violate the existing contract since no generator exists for it yet. But the doctrine says "Generated prose compiles from the graph wherever there is an oracle," which suggests knowledge/CLAUDE.md *should* be graph-compiled—so by hand-editing it, I've deepened the hand-written-prose debt rather than building the generator. There's a latent gap: the knowledge repo needs an output-target contract for CLAUDE.md.

Now I need to read the /end skill to understand the thread persistence question precisely.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: end
3	description: End-of-session sweep - persist unpersisted concepts to the right cb-dag, route direction content (strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation) to the private direction repos, audit session memory, package learnings into cb-site blog drafts, graduate scratch surfaces, verify, commit, and report the desk. Use when the user invokes "/end" or says they are wrapping up the session.
4	---
5	
6	# /end - close the session with nothing left only in the chat
7	
8	Invoking this skill is the user's authorization to commit and push the
9	persistence artifacts it produces (per repo git policy, which otherwise
10	requires explicit instruction). Destructive operations still confirm.
11	
12	Pull all touched repos before writing anything: other sessions write these
13	repos concurrently, and obligations are authored against the live graph,
14	never from memory (cb:a504).
15	
16	## Step 1 - worktree and tree hygiene
17	
18	If the session ran in a worktree, merge to main and clean up (orphans are
19	/merge-worktrees' job). Otherwise confirm working trees are clean or account
20	for every uncommitted file before proceeding.
21	
22	## Step 2 - thread sweep: what exists only in this conversation?
23	
24	Re-read the thread for: decisions made, conventions settled, findings and
25	incidents, stances argued, direction content worked through, obligations
26	created or discovered, tool facts learned, and agent reasoning errors the
27	user caught. For each, check whether it is already persisted (graph node,
28	plan record, position doc, operations note, direction doc). Route the
29	unpersisted by proportionality (cb:a496):
30	
31	- **Direction content - strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation,
32	  audience/market analysis, monetization and distribution thinking** ->
33	  NEVER a public-facing surface: not a README, not docs/, not positions/,
34	  not chronicles/, not a public graph, not a blog draft. It routes to
35	  exactly one of two private repos, by subject, as a dated doc in that
36	  repo's `direction/`:
37	  - CB-the-system (its positioning, competitors, distribution) ->
38	    `composablebeliefs/cb-direction`
39	  - amieval and everything else (the broader operation, cross-system
40	    strategy) -> `amieval/amieval-direction`
41	  These two are the only categorical direction destinations; do not create
42	  others. When a piece mixes checkable claims with strategy, split it: the
43	  checkable part graduates to the public surface, the strategic part goes
44	  to direction. Rationale on record in cb-direction's README and
45	  `direction/2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md`: publishing the
46	  strategic rationale undercuts the credibility the public positioning
47	  pursues.
48	- **Stance whose reasoning matters verbatim** -> position document (in the
49	  repo whose system it is about, per cb:a492), then extract claims into the
50	  dag through the write flow.
51	- **Finding or convention with an obvious prescription** -> directive
52	  straight into the dag (gap as rationale prose or a small design-gap node).
53	- **Obligation / future work** -> backlog directive: kind action-item,
54	  tag `lifecycle:discrete`, grounded in deps or a stipulation artifact.
55	  Verify it is not already done or already on the desk before minting.
56	- **Tool facts** (editor behaviors, CLI quirks) -> evidence detail on the
57	  relevant convention node and/or docs/operations.md. Never claims.
58	- **Agent reasoning errors caught by the user** -> error-pattern node or
59	  evidence append. Look for rejected approaches ("no, that's wrong
60	  because..."), missed existing patterns ("we already have X"), unverified
61	  assumptions, and implicit corrections (the user restating a requirement
62	  after a misread). Link to existing patterns first (`mix bs list
63	  kind:error-pattern`, `kind:reasoning-error`); a recurring shape with no
64	  match seeds a new node, subject `{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}`.
65	  Distinguish what happened from why: observed behavior is a primitive
66	  ("observed: X"); a theory about the agent's reasoning is a compound
67	  ("theory: X"). Evidence detail tells the specific story ("agent proposed
68	  concatenated location field"), never the generic one ("agent made a
69	  modeling error"). Don't over-assert: one-off slips that feed no future
70	  reasoning stay in the chat.
71	
72	All graph writes go through the front door: preflight, record the
73	tag-overlap review in the evidence detail (overlap is necessary but never
74	sufficient for conflict), import, verify. Never hand-edit a graph file in a
75	repo that has a write flow.
76	
77	## Step 3 - dag routing
78	
79	Home each belief by the boundary: claims about CB-the-system land in `cb:`
80	(composable-beliefs); mission, domain, and field-use content lands in the
81	appropriate belief-collections collection (operator field observations carry
82	the `anecdote` tag per paradigm:a368; meta-observations about the human-agent
83	system go to `paradigm:`). Discover what dags exist via `collections.json`
84	and sibling `manifest.json` files rather than assuming.
85	
86	- If content belongs to several dags and the homes are clear, write each
87	  piece to its home and say so.
88	- If the homing is ambiguous, ask the user, with a recommendation.
89	- If a touched repo carries **no dag**, tell the user explicitly. Distinguish
90	  "missing" from "no dag by design" (cb-site carries none deliberately:
91	  proto-content churns; settled claims graduate to positions later).
92	
93	## Step 4 - session memory audit
94	
95	Session memory is an ephemeral cache (cb:a509): project and work state is
96	banned from it. Scan the memory directory for anything load-bearing that
97	lives only there and promote it through the write flow (the cb:a504-a506
98	precedent). Then PRUNE: delete the project-state files, leaving MEMORY.md as
99	a one-line pointer at the graph (CLAUDE.md Session start + the desk). At
100	most a thin operator-preferences note survives, and only until the private
101	overlay collection gives preferences a graph home.
102	
103	## Step 5 - transcript, chronicle, and scratch graduation
104	
105	If the session did plan-scale work, persist or postscript its transcript in
106	the co-located plans convention (plans/<set>/transcript-execution.md).
107	
108	If the session was **decision-weight** (cb:a540) - it minted or superseded
109	beliefs grounded in a user:/session: stipulation, settled a stance, or
110	adjudicated contradictory positions - also persist the thread **verbatim**.
111	cb:a518 is now landed, so this is a **file duplication, never a
112	reconstruction**: copy the real session log - the uuid-keyed `.jsonl` for this
113	session under `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project>/` (the file whose name
114	matches this session's id, newest by mtime) - byte-for-byte into the
115	subject-appropriate repo as an immutable `type: source` doc, following the
116	knowledge standard's source+thread persistence rule
117	(amieval/knowledge `standard/types.md`). Home it by subject: strategy/direction
118	threads to the private direction repos (amieval-direction / cb-direction, by
119	the cb-direction routing rule), plan-scale dev threads to plans/<set>/. A
120	continuation of an existing arc appends to that arc's thread dir rather than
121	starting a new one. The copied `.jsonl` is what every `session:` artifact now
122	resolves to, so retro-pair each belief minted this session (cb:a507) with a
123	`document:` pointer to it. The directive stays the SSOT and self-bootstrapping;
124	the verbatim thread is provenance only.
125	
126	Then write or extend the thread's **chronicle** (cb:a520): a dated prose
127	narrative in chronicles/ of the repo where the thread centered - where
128	things stood, the arc with its incidents as story beats, where things stand
129	now, what the next session inherits. Written for the operator's steering,
130	never as a dry event list: narrative carries the load, ids stay subordinate
131	(the inverse of the receipts register). The transcript is the record; the
132	chronicle is how the human keeps up.
133	Graduate the tmp/ glue surfaces (lap-log, transcript.md, cards): anything
134	they surfaced that matters moves to the transcript or the graph; the scratch
135	files themselves stay untracked and die. Adjudication wire records and
136	proposal files graduate to the proposals/ dir of the thread's
137	center-of-gravity plan set - the graph carries the adjudication, the file
138	carries the provenance.
139	
140	After the chronicle and transcript exist, retro-pair: append a `document:`
141	pointer to any evidence minted this session that cites a bare `session:`
142	slug (cb:a507 - a session ref is a dead end for a fresh agent until the
143	persistence pipeline lands; cb:a518 is the systemic fix).
144	
145	## Step 6 - blog packaging (cb-site)
146	
147	Package learnings that apply to CB or amieval into dated drafts in
148	`cb-site/posts/drafts/` (private repo: composablebeliefs/cb-site). Instruction,
149	verbatim from the operator:
150	
151	> these are as much for me to read to understand what the functionality now
152	> is as to explain how we got here and why decisions were made as they were.
153	> this should be less of a sequential recounting and more of an actual blog
154	> post. concepts should be explained, assume an audience that does not yet
155	> know what cb is, though is technical enough to read posts with code. do
156	> not worry about writerly flourishes, do not try to "write", instead focus
157	> on relaying what you think is consequential in a straightforward style
158	
159	Separate posts by topic, never by chronology. One draft per genuinely
160	distinct learning; skip sessions that produced none (routine work is not a
161	post). Mark each as Draft with origin and audience at the top. House prose
162	rules apply: no emdashes; state what things are directly. Report performance
163	claims as anecdote until an eval says otherwise.
164	
165	Drafts are publication-bound: no direction content (strategy, positioning,
166	competitor evaluation) in a draft - that routes to the direction repos per
167	step 2, even when it would make good narrative.
168	
169	## Step 7 - verify, commit, report
170	
171	For every touched repo, run its gates before committing: `mix test`,
172	`mix cb.verify.schema`, `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check`,
173	`mix cb.verify.collection <ns>` for touched collections. Leave green or
174	report exactly what is red and why.
175	
176	Commit per repo with messages that say what landed and why; push. Close with
177	the session report:
178	
179	- what was persisted where (ids, files, commits)
180	- the live desk: `mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete` per relevant
181	  graph (note the cross-collection gap, cb:a500, until it is built)
182	- anything that needs the user: ambiguous homings, red gates, decisions
183	  deferred
184	
185	## Step 8 - the close sweeps itself
186	
187	The close itself surfaces findings: calibration issues, machinery friction,
188	skill stumbles, observations "worth carrying forward." Persist them before
189	reporting them - a finding that lives only in the final chat message is the
190	exact failure this skill exists to prevent. Route by step 2's rules (usually
191	a desk directive or an evidence append on the node it concerns), then let
192	the report reference the id.

file.content:
---
name: end
description: End-of-session sweep - persist unpersisted concepts to the right cb-dag, route direction content (strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation) to the private direction repos, audit session memory, package learnings into cb-site blog drafts, graduate scratch surfaces, verify, commit, and report the desk. Use when the user invokes "/end" or says they are wrapping up the session.
---

# /end - close the session with nothing left only in the chat

Invoking this skill is the user's authorization to commit and push the
persistence artifacts it produces (per repo git policy, which otherwise
requires explicit instruction). Destructive operations still confirm.

Pull all touched repos before writing anything: other sessions write these
repos concurrently, and obligations are authored against the live graph,
never from memory (cb:a504).

## Step 1 - worktree and tree hygiene

If the session ran in a worktree, merge to main and clean up (orphans are
/merge-worktrees' job). Otherwise confirm working trees are clean or account
for every uncommitted file before proceeding.

## Step 2 - thread sweep: what exists only in this conversation?

Re-read the thread for: decisions made, conventions settled, findings and
incidents, stances argued, direction content worked through, obligations
created or discovered, tool facts learned, and agent reasoning errors the
user caught. For each, check whether it is already persisted (graph node,
plan record, position doc, operations note, direction doc). Route the
unpersisted by proportionality (cb:a496):

- **Direction content - strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation,
  audience/market analysis, monetization and distribution thinking** ->
  NEVER a public-facing surface: not a README, not docs/, not positions/,
  not chronicles/, not a public graph, not a blog draft. It routes to
  exactly one of two private repos, by subject, as a dated doc in that
  repo's `direction/`:
  - CB-the-system (its positioning, competitors, distribution) ->
    `composablebeliefs/cb-direction`
  - amieval and everything else (the broader operation, cross-system
    strategy) -> `amieval/amieval-direction`
  These two are the only categorical direction destinations; do not create
  others. When a piece mixes checkable claims with strategy, split it: the
  checkable part graduates to the public surface, the strategic part goes
  to direction. Rationale on record in cb-direction's README and
  `direction/2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md`: publishing the
  strategic rationale undercuts the credibility the public positioning
  pursues.
- **Stance whose reasoning matters verbatim** -> position document (in the
  repo whose system it is about, per cb:a492), then extract claims into the
  dag through the write flow.
- **Finding or convention with an obvious prescription** -> directive
  straight into the dag (gap as rationale prose or a small design-gap node).
- **Obligation / future work** -> backlog directive: kind action-item,
  tag `lifecycle:discrete`, grounded in deps or a stipulation artifact.
  Verify it is not already done or already on the desk before minting.
- **Tool facts** (editor behaviors, CLI quirks) -> evidence detail on the
  relevant convention node and/or docs/operations.md. Never claims.
- **Agent reasoning errors caught by the user** -> error-pattern node or
  evidence append. Look for rejected approaches ("no, that's wrong
  because..."), missed existing patterns ("we already have X"), unverified
  assumptions, and implicit corrections (the user restating a requirement
  after a misread). Link to existing patterns first (`mix bs list
  kind:error-pattern`, `kind:reasoning-error`); a recurring shape with no
  match seeds a new node, subject `{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}`.
  Distinguish what happened from why: observed behavior is a primitive
  ("observed: X"); a theory about the agent's reasoning is a compound
  ("theory: X"). Evidence detail tells the specific story ("agent proposed
  concatenated location field"), never the generic one ("agent made a
  modeling error"). Don't over-assert: one-off slips that feed no future
  reasoning stay in the chat.

All graph writes go through the front door: preflight, record the
tag-overlap review in the evidence detail (overlap is necessary but never
sufficient for conflict), import, verify. Never hand-edit a graph file in a
repo that has a write flow.

## Step 3 - dag routing

Home each belief by the boundary: claims about CB-the-system land in `cb:`
(composable-beliefs); mission, domain, and field-use content lands in the
appropriate belief-collections collection (operator field observations carry
the `anecdote` tag per paradigm:a368; meta-observations about the human-agent
system go to `paradigm:`). Discover what dags exist via `collections.json`
and sibling `manifest.json` files rather than assuming.

- If content belongs to several dags and the homes are clear, write each
  piece to its home and say so.
- If the homing is ambiguous, ask the user, with a recommendation.
- If a touched repo carries **no dag**, tell the user explicitly. Distinguish
  "missing" from "no dag by design" (cb-site carries none deliberately:
  proto-content churns; settled claims graduate to positions later).

## Step 4 - session memory audit

Session memory is an ephemeral cache (cb:a509): project and work state is
banned from it. Scan the memory directory for anything load-bearing that
lives only there and promote it through the write flow (the cb:a504-a506
precedent). Then PRUNE: delete the project-state files, leaving MEMORY.md as
a one-line pointer at the graph (CLAUDE.md Session start + the desk). At
most a thin operator-preferences note survives, and only until the private
overlay collection gives preferences a graph home.

## Step 5 - transcript, chronicle, and scratch graduation

If the session did plan-scale work, persist or postscript its transcript in
the co-located plans convention (plans/<set>/transcript-execution.md).

If the session was **decision-weight** (cb:a540) - it minted or superseded
beliefs grounded in a user:/session: stipulation, settled a stance, or
adjudicated contradictory positions - also persist the thread **verbatim**.
cb:a518 is now landed, so this is a **file duplication, never a
reconstruction**: copy the real session log - the uuid-keyed `.jsonl` for this
session under `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project>/` (the file whose name
matches this session's id, newest by mtime) - byte-for-byte into the
subject-appropriate repo as an immutable `type: source` doc, following the
knowledge standard's source+thread persistence rule
(amieval/knowledge `standard/types.md`). Home it by subject: strategy/direction
threads to the private direction repos (amieval-direction / cb-direction, by
the cb-direction routing rule), plan-scale dev threads to plans/<set>/. A
continuation of an existing arc appends to that arc's thread dir rather than
starting a new one. The copied `.jsonl` is what every `session:` artifact now
resolves to, so retro-pair each belief minted this session (cb:a507) with a
`document:` pointer to it. The directive stays the SSOT and self-bootstrapping;
the verbatim thread is provenance only.

Then write or extend the thread's **chronicle** (cb:a520): a dated prose
narrative in chronicles/ of the repo where the thread centered - where
things stood, the arc with its incidents as story beats, where things stand
now, what the next session inherits. Written for the operator's steering,
never as a dry event list: narrative carries the load, ids stay subordinate
(the inverse of the receipts register). The transcript is the record; the
chronicle is how the human keeps up.
Graduate the tmp/ glue surfaces (lap-log, transcript.md, cards): anything
they surfaced that matters moves to the transcript or the graph; the scratch
files themselves stay untracked and die. Adjudication wire records and
proposal files graduate to the proposals/ dir of the thread's
center-of-gravity plan set - the graph carries the adjudication, the file
carries the provenance.

After the chronicle and transcript exist, retro-pair: append a `document:`
pointer to any evidence minted this session that cites a bare `session:`
slug (cb:a507 - a session ref is a dead end for a fresh agent until the
persistence pipeline lands; cb:a518 is the systemic fix).

## Step 6 - blog packaging (cb-site)

Package learnings that apply to CB or amieval into dated drafts in
`cb-site/posts/drafts/` (private repo: composablebeliefs/cb-site). Instruction,
verbatim from the operator:

> these are as much for me to read to understand what the functionality now
> is as to explain how we got here and why decisions were made as they were.
> this should be less of a sequential recounting and more of an actual blog
> post. concepts should be explained, assume an audience that does not yet
> know what cb is, though is technical enough to read posts with code. do
> not worry about writerly flourishes, do not try to "write", instead focus
> on relaying what you think is consequential in a straightforward style

Separate posts by topic, never by chronology. One draft per genuinely
distinct learning; skip sessions that produced none (routine work is not a
post). Mark each as Draft with origin and audience at the top. House prose
rules apply: no emdashes; state what things are directly. Report performance
claims as anecdote until an eval says otherwise.

Drafts are publication-bound: no direction content (strategy, positioning,
competitor evaluation) in a draft - that routes to the direction repos per
step 2, even when it would make good narrative.

## Step 7 - verify, commit, report

For every touched repo, run its gates before committing: `mix test`,
`mix cb.verify.schema`, `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check`,
`mix cb.verify.collection <ns>` for touched collections. Leave green or
report exactly what is red and why.

Commit per repo with messages that say what landed and why; push. Close with
the session report:

- what was persisted where (ids, files, commits)
- the live desk: `mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete` per relevant
  graph (note the cross-collection gap, cb:a500, until it is built)
- anything that needs the user: ambiguous homings, red gates, decisions
  deferred

## Step 8 - the close sweeps itself

The close itself surfaces findings: calibration issues, machinery friction,
skill stumbles, observations "worth carrying forward." Persist them before
reporting them - a finding that lives only in the final chat message is the
exact failure this skill exists to prevent. Route by step 2's rules (usually
a desk directive or an evidence append on the node it concerns), then let
the report reference the id.

content.thinking:
I'm pulling together the full picture now. The document is `meta/2026-06-22-elixir-only-collapse.md` with type `thread`, and the user's understanding about thread persistence is accurate—threads save at session end via the /end skill, not during the session, with decision-weight sessions following their own persistence rules.

My hand-authored thread summary actually violates the operational policy in a few ways: I created it mid-session rather than at /end, I didn't pair it with the verbatim `.jsonl` source file as the knowledge standard requires, and I skipped the chronicle narrative that should accompany it. The doctrine emphasizes the verbatim source as the primary artifact with a prose chronicle, not a lossy hand-written summary created in isolation.

The cleaner approach is to remove that premature thread doc and let /end handle persistence correctly—capturing the verbatim transcript plus chronicle at session close. The actual decisions and follow-up work (port regeneration, shedding to_contract) should live as backlog directives in the knowledge graph instead, which is the proper place for actionable obligations. I should propose reverting the thread doc and letting the graph capture what matters. I'm deciding on the right approach: answer the questions directly, fix the test (clearly authorized), use the beliefs permission to capture the obligations properly, and then handle the thread doc. The user's tone signals they're correcting a pattern, so removing the premature doc aligns with the stated policy—I'll do it and report clearly so they can object if needed. I need to check the knowledge write flow to assert these obligations correctly. I need to reconsider the CLAUDE.md policy more carefully. The composable-beliefs CLAUDE.md is governed by contract c061 and regenerated by `mix cb.generate.claude_md`, so it's read-only and hand-edits get overwritten. I didn't touch it, which is correct. But knowledge/CLAUDE.md is different — I hand-edited it, and while there's no generator currently wired to it, the doctrine says it *should* be graph-compiled from the knowledge: graph. So I didn't violate an existing policy, but my edit entrenches a gap that should be fixed by making knowledge/CLAUDE.md a proper generated output-target. That's a real backlog item that ties back to the governance framework.

The irony is sharp: the operational directive I added about not running OKF tools on beliefs/ should itself live as a belief in the graph and be rendered, not hand-written into CLAUDE.md — that's exactly what the standard's own §1.1 says about operational directives being owned by the consumer. So I violated the knowledge standard's owns-vs-consumer rule by hand-writing that directive. Now I need to read the test and check the write-flow task options to move forward efficiently.

defmodule CB.SchemaContractsTest do
  @moduledoc """
  Dogfooded verification suite for the self-referential schema contracts.

  Unlike `CB.Belief.ContractTest` (which exercises the interpreters against
  inline fixtures), this suite loads the *reshaped* schema contracts straight
  from the `cb:` belief graph (`beliefs/beliefs.json`), drives them through
  the contract interpreters, and asserts that `CB.Belief` and the live graph
  conform. This is the `verify_against_contract` pattern: the contract in the
  graph is the source of truth, and the code is checked against it.

  Covered:

  - cb:c053 (state-machine): the StateMachine-derived state set equals
    `CB.Belief.statuses()`, and each edge's `requires` matches the expected
    linkage slugs.
  - cb:c039 / cb:c043 / cb:c041 (enum-registry): `Enum.values_for/2` returns the
    expected vocabularies, and every active belief's kind / artifact-scheme /
    domain in the graph is a declared value (`valid_value?/3`). There is no
    `CB.Belief` code list for kind / domain / scheme - the contract is the SSOT.
    (cb:c043 superseded cb:c040 when the `code` scheme was added; the linkage
    is pinned here too.)
  """
  use ExUnit.Case, async: true

  alias CB.Belief
  alias CB.Belief.Store
  alias CB.Belief.Contract.{Enum, StateMachine}

  # Load the live graph once. Tests read the reshaped contracts from it.
  setup_all do
    {:ok, all} = Store.read()
    by_id = Map.new(all, &{&1.id, &1})
    {:ok, all: all, by_id: by_id}
  end

  defp fetch(by_id, id) do
    case Map.fetch(by_id, id) do
      {:ok, b} -> b
      :error -> flunk("contract #{id} not found in the belief graph")
    end
  end

  # Scheme of an artifact URI ("session:2026-..." -> "session").
  defp scheme(uri) when is_binary(uri) do
    case String.split(uri, ":", parts: 2) do
      [s, _] -> s
      _ -> ""
    end
  end

  describe "cb:c053 status lifecycle (state-machine)" do
    test "is an active state-machine contract", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c053 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c053")
      assert c053.kind == "state-machine"
      assert c053.status == "active"
      assert c053.contract == true
    end

    test "StateMachine-derived state set equals CB.Belief.statuses/0", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c053 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c053")

      derived =
        c053
        |> StateMachine.edges()
        |> Elixir.Enum.flat_map(fn e -> [e.from, e.to] end)
        |> Elixir.Enum.uniq()
        |> Elixir.Enum.sort()

      assert derived == Elixir.Enum.sort(Belief.statuses())
    end

    test "requires/2 returns the expected linkage slugs for each edge", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c053 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c053")

      assert StateMachine.requires(c053, {"active", "superseded"}) == {:ok, ["superseded_by"]}

      assert StateMachine.requires(c053, {"active", "retracted"}) ==
               {:ok, ["retracted_on", "retracted_reason"]}

      assert StateMachine.requires(c053, {"active", "retired"}) == {:ok, []}
    end

    test "all transitions originate from the active state", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c053 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c053")

      froms =
        c053 |> StateMachine.edges() |> Elixir.Enum.map(& &1.from) |> Elixir.Enum.uniq()

      assert froms == ["active"]
    end

    test "terminal states have no outgoing transitions", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c053 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c053")

      for terminal <- ~w(superseded retracted retired) do
        assert StateMachine.transitions_from(c053, terminal) == [],
               "expected #{terminal} to be terminal"
      end
    end

    test "every belief in the graph has a status the contract admits", %{
      all: all,
      by_id: by_id
    } do
      c053 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c053")

      admitted =
        c053
        |> StateMachine.edges()
        |> Elixir.Enum.flat_map(fn e -> [e.from, e.to] end)
        |> MapSet.new()

      bad =
        all
        |> Elixir.Enum.reject(&MapSet.member?(admitted, &1.status))
        |> Elixir.Enum.map(&{&1.id, &1.status})

      assert bad == []
    end
  end

  describe "cb:c039 kind enum-registry" do
    @expected_kinds [
      "policy",
      "rule",
      "action-item",
      "fact",
      "observation",
      "error",
      "error-pattern",
      "reasoning-error",
      "schema",
      "state-machine",
      "derivation-rule",
      "design-principle",
      "design-rationale",
      "design-observation",
      "design-property",
      "design-gap",
      "convention",
      "domain-rule",
      "domain-enum",
      "composable-belief",
      "agent-architecture",
      "analogical-claim",
      "architectural-synthesis",
      "audit-rule",
      "derivation-table",
      "edit-pair",
      "feedback-loop",
      "formatting-rule",
      "governance",
      "human-factor",
      "meta-observation",
      "outcome-claim",
      "output-target",
      "structural-parallel",
      "training-distribution",
      "training-incentive",
      "definition",
      "enum-registry"
    ]

    test "is an active enum-registry contract", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c039 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c039")
      assert c039.kind == "enum-registry"
      assert c039.status == "active"
    end

    test "values_for/2 returns the expected kind vocabulary", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c039 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c039")
      assert Enum.values_for(c039, "kind") == @expected_kinds
    end

    test "the self-referential enum-registry value is declared", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c039 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c039")
      assert Enum.valid_value?(c039, "kind", "enum-registry")
    end

    test "every active belief's kind is a declared value", %{all: all, by_id: by_id} do
      c039 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c039")

      bad =
        all
        |> Elixir.Enum.filter(&(&1.status == "active" and not is_nil(&1.kind)))
        |> Elixir.Enum.reject(&Enum.valid_value?(c039, "kind", &1.kind))
        |> Elixir.Enum.map(&{&1.id, &1.kind})

      assert bad == []
    end
  end

  describe "cb:c043 artifact-scheme enum-registry (supersedes cb:c040)" do
    @expected_schemes ["gmail", "session", "user", "document", "source", "https", "plan", "code"]

    test "is an active enum-registry contract", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c043 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c043")
      assert c043.kind == "enum-registry"
      assert c043.status == "active"
    end

    test "the superseded cb:c040 links forward to cb:c043", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c040 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c040")
      assert c040.status == "superseded"
      assert c040.superseded_by == "cb:c043"
    end

    test "values_for/2 returns the expected scheme vocabulary including code", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c043 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c043")
      assert Enum.values_for(c043, "artifact-scheme") == @expected_schemes
      assert Enum.valid_value?(c043, "artifact-scheme", "code")
      assert Enum.valid_value?(c043, "artifact-scheme", "session")
    end

    test "every active belief's artifact scheme is a declared value", %{all: all, by_id: by_id} do
      c043 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c043")

      bad =
        all
        |> Elixir.Enum.filter(&(&1.status == "active" and is_binary(&1.artifact)))
        |> Elixir.Enum.reject(&Enum.valid_value?(c043, "artifact-scheme", scheme(&1.artifact)))
        |> Elixir.Enum.map(&{&1.id, &1.artifact})

      assert bad == []
    end
  end

  describe "cb:c041 domain enum-registry" do
    @expected_domains ["system", "design", "agent", "ops", "dev"]

    test "is an active enum-registry contract", %{by_id: by_id} do
      c041 = fetch(by_id, "cb:c041")
